title: Dog Ate Garden Fertilizer: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate garden fertilizer, understand the risks, symptoms, and when to seek emergency veterinary care. Read our helpful triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: Fertilizer ingestion in dogs is generally a medium-level emergency that can range from mild stomach upset to severe toxicity depending on the ingredients. Contact your veterinarian immediately and have the product packaging or a photo of the ingredients ready for triage.

What happens if my dog eats fertilizer?

Fertilizer ingestion occurs when a dog consumes plant food or soil additives. These products typically contain various levels of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. While these primary ingredients are often just irritating to the stomach, many garden fertilizers also contain bone meal, blood meal, or chemical additives like pesticides and herbicides that can be significantly more harmful if ingested.

Is it an emergency if my dog ate fertilizer?

  • The urgency level for fertilizer ingestion is Medium.
  • While a small lick or taste may only cause mild gastrointestinal upset, eating large quantities or products with specialized additives can lead to serious complications.
  • Because some fertilizers contain iron or organophosphates which are highly toxic, you should contact your veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately to determine if your dog needs to be seen.

Why should I take a photo of the fertilizer packaging for my vet?

  • Take a clear photo of the fertilizer bag, focusing specifically on the ingredients list and the guaranteed analysis (the N-P-K ratio).
  • This visual information is vital for veterinary triage as it allows the medical team to identify specific toxic components quickly.
  • Accurate identification helps the vet decide whether your dog requires decontamination, such as induced vomiting or supportive care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of garden fertilizer by a dog, triage should include assessing the type and amount of fertilizer ingested, as specific ingredients (e.g., elemental iron, coal tar derivatives, grapes/raisins) necessitate tailored interventions. For significant ingestions of grapes or raisins, emesis should be induced with 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg; not exceeding 45 mL), followed by activated charcoal; aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended if a large amount was ingested, or if vomiting and/or diarrhea have already developed within 12 hours. In cases of elemental iron ingestion exceeding 20 mg/kg, emesis should be induced, and GI protectants administered, with activated charcoal being ineffective. For iron ingestion exceeding 60 mg/kg, further treatment and monitoring are required; milk of magnesia (magnesium hydroxide; 5-30 mL once or twice daily) can reduce iron absorption. Serum iron levels and total serum iron binding capacity should be evaluated at 3 and 8-10 hours post-exposure, with chelation therapy considered if serum iron exceeds 300 mcg/dL along with clinical signs, or if it surpasses the total iron binding capacity. For coal-tar product poisoning, emetics and gastric lavage are not recommended, but activated charcoal and saline cathartics may reduce absorption. Consider administering egg whites to dilute and bind phenols. Monitor for hypoglycemia, elevated serum liver enzymes, and kidney damage.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3046)

faqs.question: What symptoms should I look for after my dog eats fertilizer?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. More severe signs to watch for include muscle tremors, seizures, or extreme lethargy, which indicate a more toxic exposure.

faqs.question: Is organic fertilizer safe for pets?
faqs.answer: Organic does not mean non-toxic. Organic fertilizers often use bone, blood, or fish meal, which are highly attractive to dogs. If eaten in large amounts, these can form a concrete-like obstruction in the stomach or cause severe pancreatitis.

faqs.question: Can fertilizer cause long-term damage?
faqs.answer: Most cases result in short-term stomach upset, but fertilizers containing heavy metals like iron or concentrated chemicals can lead to liver or kidney damage if not treated promptly.
